proposal.reasoning: The diagnostic reveals a critical anti-correlation: density is underestimated by ~11% (indicating weak cohesion or inefficient packing) while surface tension is overestimated by ~12-16% (indicating excessive dipolar alignment at the interface). This suggests the current parameters have suboptimal coupling between LJ and electrostatic terms. Based on the recommendations, I propose 8 forks that systematically explore: (1) reducing charge magnitudes to 0.22-0.24 to lower surface tension, (2) maximizing dummy bond lengths (1.34 Å) to compensate dipole moment when charges are reduced, (3) increasing |ε| toward -0.42 to strengthen bulk cohesion for density, (4) testing both tight Rmin (5.18 Å) for density and large Rmin (5.3 Å) for surface tension compensation, and (5) varying geometry between linear (θ₀=140°) for packing efficiency and bent (θ₀=130°) to disrupt interfacial ordering. Forks 1-4 test aggressive combinations of low charge/high ε with different geometries. Fork 5 tests large Rmin with strong ε. Forks 6-8 provide conservative and intermediate variations to map the local response surface around the current best (f=28.26).

hypothesis.scientific_rationale: Reducing charge magnitudes from ±0.24 to ±0.22-0.23 will decrease excessive dipole-dipole interactions at the interface, thereby lowering the overestimated surface tension. To maintain the target dipole moment of 3.96 D despite reduced charges, dummy bond lengths are maximized to 1.33-1.34 Å (within the 1.34 Å boundary). Simultaneously, increasing |ε| from 0.395 to 0.40-0.42 strengthens van der Waals cohesive energy to address the 11% density deficit, while contracting Rmin to 5.18-5.20 Å improves packing efficiency. Increasing θ₀ toward 140° linearizes the geometry for better bulk packing, while reducing kθ to 20-30 increases flexibility to disrupt ordered surface layers.
hypothesis.expected_benefit: This decoupling strategy will simultaneously increase bulk density (via stronger LJ and tighter packing) and decrease surface tension (via reduced interfacial dipolar alignment), resolving the current anti-correlated errors. The heat of vaporization should increase toward target due to stronger ε, while the dipole moment remains matched to 3.96 D via the charge-bond length compensation.
hypothesis.test_method: Evaluate all 8 parameter sets in parallel MD simulations at 298K and 323K. Monitor for: (1) density convergence via Rmin contraction and ε increase, (2) surface tension reduction via charge lowering and geometric flexibility, (3) dipole moment stability via bond length compensation, and (4) HVap balance via the ε-charge trade-off. Compare Forks 1 (tight Rmin) vs 5 (large Rmin) to determine the optimal size-cohesion balance.

proposal.reasoning: The diagnostic reveals a critical conflict: density is underestimated by ~13% (requiring tighter packing/stronger cohesion) while surface tension is overestimated by 23% (requiring weaker polarity/reduced cohesion). Current best parameters (charge=0.24, Rmin=5.2/2.78, epsilon=-0.395) represent a compromise that fails to resolve both issues simultaneously. Since we're in exploration phase, I propose 8 forks that systematically decouple these competing requirements by: (1) reducing Rmin to the boundary (5.18/2.75) to maximize density gains, (2) varying charges from 0.22-0.24 to tune surface tension, (3) testing epsilon from -0.38 to -0.42 to find the optimal balance between bulk cohesion and interfacial tension, and (4) reducing dummy bond lengths (0.9-1.3) to lower dipole moments. Forks 1-3 test the extremes of the epsilon-charge-Rmin trade space. Forks 4-5 test intermediate Rmin values with aggressive charge/bond reductions. Fork 6 tests geometric flexibility (lower ktheta, higher theta0) as an alternative mechanism to reduce surface tension. Fork 7 tests stiffer angles with moderate parameters. Fork 8 provides a refined intermediate. This design maps the response surface around the diagnostic recommendations while respecting physical constraints.

hypothesis.scientific_rationale: Reducing charge magnitudes from ±0.24 to ±0.22-0.23 weakens dipolar contributions that dominate the excessive surface tension (+22.85%) without significantly compromising bulk density, as van der Waals interactions (epsilon) and geometric packing (Rmin) can compensate. Simultaneously contracting Rmin from 5.2/2.78 Å to 5.18/2.75 Å increases packing density to address the 13% density deficit. Shortening dummy bond lengths from 1.3 Å to 0.9-1.2 Å reduces the DUP-DUN separation distance, directly lowering the molecular dipole moment and thus surface tension. Varying epsilon between -0.38 and -0.42 tests whether weaker LJ interactions (less negative epsilon) can maintain adequate bulk cohesion through tighter packing (small Rmin) while reducing interfacial tension, or whether stronger epsilon is needed to compensate for reduced charges.
hypothesis.expected_benefit: This decoupling strategy will simultaneously increase density (via Rmin contraction) and decrease surface tension (via charge reduction and shorter dummy bonds), resolving the current anti-correlation. The epsilon variation will identify whether the system requires stronger van der Waals forces to maintain HVap (currently excellent at 298K) or whether weaker interactions better balance the reduced electrostatics. Geometric variations (theta0/ktheta) test whether molecular flexibility or linearity can further decouple bulk from interfacial properties.
hypothesis.test_method: Evaluate all 8 parameter sets in parallel MD simulations at 298K and 323K. Monitor density convergence via Rmin contraction effects, surface tension reduction via charge/bond lowering, and HVap stability via epsilon tuning. Track dipole moment changes to validate the dummy bond length hypothesis.
